summary: McKenzie documents a Paypal bug where he couldn't withdraw money despite having already confirmed his primary email address. The system simultaneously told him he needed to confirm his email and that it was already confirmed. He found a workaround by adding a new disposable email address to the account, which resolved the withdrawal block without disrupting his payment notifications. He shares the fix publicly hoping Google will surface it for others stuck in the same catch-22.
key_points: Paypal had a bug creating a catch-22: withdrawal required email confirmation, but the email was already confirmed and couldn't be re-confirmed
The workaround was adding a new disposable email address to the account, which unblocked withdrawals without changing the primary address
McKenzie notes the dread of having to contact Paypal customer service, reflecting the notoriously poor support experience
He left the disposable address in place rather than removing it immediately, fearing rapid account changes could trigger a fraud review
money_quotes: God knows a Paypal customer with a problem has not a friend in the world.
I faced the unpleasant prospect of having to communicate with Paypal customer service — oh no!
I have the totally unsubstantiated worry that adding and removing addresses in a rapid fashion could cause a fraud review.
key_insight: Payment platforms like Paypal can trap users in absurd catch-22 situations where the only escape is creative workarounds, since customer support is effectively unreachable.
